title: Interpret Division of Fractions
contentObjective: I can use models to interpret what it means to divide by a fraction.
languageObjective: I can explain a fraction division using the words dividend, divisor, quotient, reciprocal, and unit fraction.

turnAndTalk.question: Agent Reyes cuts a 3-foot strip of tape into 1/4-foot sections. Before calculating, will the number of sections be more or less than 3? Why?
turnAndTalk.kernel: There will be more than 3 sections because each piece is smaller than a whole foot.

stems.en: The number of sections will be ___ than 3 because ___.
stems.es: El número de secciones será ___ que 3 porque ___.

stems.en: Each 1/4-foot piece is ___ than one whole foot, so ___.
stems.es: Cada pieza de 1/4 de pie es ___ que un pie entero, así que ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students reason that dividing by a number less than 1 gives a quotient greater than the dividend, so there are more than 3 sections.
turnAndTalk.extend: Predict how the number of sections would change if each piece were 1/8 foot instead of 1/4 foot. Justify your prediction.
extendStems: With 1/8-foot pieces there would be ___ sections because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: What does 3 ÷ 1/4 actually mean? Show your partner with the bar model.
turnAndTalk.kernel: 3 ÷ 1/4 asks how many 1/4-size pieces fit into 3.

stems.en: Dividing 3 by 1/4 means finding how many ___ fit into ___.
stems.es: Dividir 3 entre 1/4 significa encontrar cuántos ___ caben en ___.

stems.en: My bar model shows ___ fourths, so the quotient is ___.
stems.es: Mi modelo de barras muestra ___ cuartos, así que el cociente es ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students name 3 as the dividend and 1/4 as the divisor, and explain the model shows 12 fourths fit into 3.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why is 3 ÷ 1/4 NOT the same as 1/4 of 3? Critique a classmate who confuses the two.
extendStems: Taking 1/4 of 3 gives ___, but dividing gives ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Where in cooking, sharing, or building might you divide a whole by a fraction?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Any time you ask 'how many small pieces fit' you are dividing by a fraction.

stems.en: In real life I divide by a fraction when ___.
stems.es: En la vida real divido entre una fracción cuando ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students give a 'how many fit' scenario and correctly identify the whole as the dividend and the piece size as the divisor.
turnAndTalk.extend: Generalize: how can you tell from the words of a problem that you should divide by a fraction rather than multiply?
extendStems: The clue that tells me to divide is ___.
If the problem instead asked for ___, I would multiply because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Interpret Division of Fractions, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.

stems.en: Dividing 3 by 1/4 means finding how many ___ fit into ___.
stems.es: Dividir 3 entre 1/4 significa encontrar cuántos ___ caben en ___.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.NS.1 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

vocabulary.definition: The number you are splitting up in a division problem.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número que estás repartiendo en una división.
vocabulary.visual: In 3 ÷ 1/4 = 12, the dividend is 3 — it is the total being split
vocabulary.cloze: In 6 ÷ 1/2, the number 6 being divided is the ___.

vocabulary.definition: The number you split by in a division problem.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número entre el que divides en una división.
vocabulary.visual: In 3 ÷ 1/4 = 12, the divisor is 1/4 — it is the size of each piece
vocabulary.cloze: In 6 ÷ 1/2, the number 1/2 we divide by is the ___.

vocabulary.visual: In 3 ÷ 1/4 = 12, the quotient is 12 — there are 12 quarter-size pieces in 3
vocabulary.cloze: The answer to a division problem is the ___.

vocabulary.definitionEs: Una fracción volteada de arriba abajo.
vocabulary.visual: The reciprocal of 1/4 is 4/1 = 4. Multiplying by the reciprocal gives the same result as dividing.
vocabulary.cloze: The fraction you get by flipping the numerator and denominator is the ___.

examples.why: The reciprocal flips the fraction; it is not the same fraction.

vocabulary.definition: A fraction with 1 on top, like 1/4.
vocabulary.visual: 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5 — each represents one equal part of a whole
vocabulary.cloze: A fraction with a numerator of 1, like 1/5, is a ___.

launch.narrative: Agent Reyes finds a 3-foot strip of tape at the crime scene. The lab needs the tape cut into 1/4-foot sections for analysis. How many sections can be made? The answer cracks the code to the locked evidence vault.
launch.contextImage: Detective examining a long strip of evidence tape with a ruler and magnifying glass
noticePrompts: What total length is being divided?
What is the size of each section?
Will the answer be greater than or less than 3?
wonderPrompts: What does it mean to divide by a fraction?
Why does dividing by 1/4 give a bigger number?

conceptIntro.heading: What does it mean to divide by a fraction?
conceptIntro.intro: Dividing by a fraction asks one question: how many of that small piece fit inside the whole amount? For 3 ÷ 1/4, we ask how many 1/4-foot pieces fit into 3 feet.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: I want to cut a 3-foot strip of tape into 1/4-foot pieces. I ask: how many 1/4-foot pieces fit into 3 feet?
First I look at just 1 foot. How many 1/4-foot pieces fit in 1 foot? Four, because 4 fourths make one whole.
Now I have 3 feet. So I do 3 groups of 4: 3 × 4 = 12.
So 3 ÷ 1/4 = 12. There are 12 pieces. The answer is bigger than 3 because each piece is very small.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Let's try 2 ÷ 1/3. How many 1/3-size pieces fit into 2?
How many thirds are in 1 whole? Three.
We have 2 wholes, so 2 × 3 = 6. That means 2 ÷ 1/3 = 6.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Now use the bar model to show how many 1/4-pieces fit into 3 wholes, then move on to the practice problems.

explore.instructions: Use the bar model to see how many 1/4-size pieces fit into 3 wholes.
explore.label: Model 3 ÷ 1/4 with a bar model
explore.questionText: How many 1/4-size pieces fit into 3 wholes?

discourse.prompt: Why does dividing by a unit fraction always give a number larger than the dividend?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Dividing by 1/4 means finding how many ___ fit into ___, so the quotient is ___ than the dividend.

optional.stem: Agent Cole has 8 feet of evidence rope and cuts it into 1/3-foot pieces for tagging. What does 8 ÷ 1/3 mean?
choices: How many 1/3-foot pieces fit into 8 feet
8 feet shortened by 1/3 of a foot
8 pieces that are each 1/3 foot long
optional.explanation: Dividing by 1/3 asks how many 1/3-foot pieces fit into 8 feet. Each foot holds 3 thirds, so 8 × 3 = 24 pieces.

optional.stem: Picture clue: a 5-inch fingerprint strip is split into 1/6-inch slivers under the microscope. Which division does the picture show, and what is the count?
optional.explanation: How many 1/6-inch slivers fit into 5 inches? Each inch holds 6 sixths, so 5 × 6 = 30 slivers.

pairs.term: A 9-yard trail of footprints is marked off in 1/3-yard steps.
A 7-foot crime-scene barrier is taped into 1/4-foot zones.
A 10-cup batch of fingerprint powder is bottled in 1/2-cup jars.
A 6-foot wire of evidence is cut into 1/6-foot samples.
An 8-meter surveillance reel is logged in 1/5-meter clips.

optional.label: Match each detective story to the fraction-division expression and quotient that solves it.

optional.instructions: A detective must label each story: is the question asking 'how many groups (pieces)?' or 'how big is each share?'

items.text: How many 1/4-foot pieces fit into 9 feet of tape? (9 ÷ 1/4)
How many 1/3-yard steps fit into 9 yards of footprints? (9 ÷ 1/3)
How many 1/5-meter clips are in an 8-meter reel? (8 ÷ 1/5)
6 feet of wire shared equally among 3 agents — how long is each share?
12 photos sorted equally into 4 case files — how many per file?
20 minutes of footage split evenly across 5 suspects — how long is each clip?

categories.label: Asking: How Big Is Each Share?

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Which Question Is Being Asked?

workedExample.label: Case file
workedExample.work: A 9-foot strip of evidence tape is cut into 1/4-foot pieces. How many pieces?

workedExample.label: Detective writes
workedExample.work: This is 1/4 ÷ 9 — split the 1/4-foot piece among 9 sections.

optional.correctWork: The story asks 'how many 1/4-foot pieces fit into 9 feet,' so the dividend is the whole (9) and the divisor is the piece size (1/4): 9 ÷ 1/4. The detective swapped the dividend and divisor. The correct answer is 9 × 4 = 36 pieces.
hints: Which number is the whole amount being cut up, and which is the size of each small piece?
The dividend is the total you start with (9 feet); the divisor is the size of each piece (1/4 foot) — do not flip them.

approaching.explanation: 3 ÷ 1/4 asks: how many 1/4-size pieces fit into 3? The answer is 12, because each whole has 4 fourths, and 3 × 4 = 12.

approaching.stem: Which expression means 'how many 1/3-size pieces are in 2'?
approaching.explanation: Finding how many 1/3-size pieces fit into 2 is represented by 2 ÷ 1/3. The answer is 6.

approaching.stem: A rope is 4 feet long. How many 1/2-foot pieces can be cut from it?
approaching.explanation: 4 ÷ 1/2 = 4 × 2 = 8 pieces. Each whole foot contains 2 halves, and 4 × 2 = 8.

approaching.instructions: Sort each statement: does dividing by the fraction give a result GREATER than the dividend, or LESS than the dividend?

approaching.explanation: 6 ÷ 1/5 means how many 1/5-size pieces fit into 6. Each whole has 5 fifths, so 6 × 5 = 30.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Interpret Division of Fractions.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.

onLevel.label: Match each division expression to its quotient.

onLevel.instructions: Use the bar model to show how many 1/6-size pieces fit into 1 whole. Then determine how many fit into 3 wholes.
onLevel.label: Model 3 ÷ 1/6 with a bar model
onLevel.questionText: How many 1/6-size pieces fit into 3 wholes?

onLevel.instructions: Plot the quotient of each expression on the number line.
onLevel.label: Place each result on the number line

onLevel.stem: A shelf is 5 feet long. Books are each 1/4 foot wide. How many books fit on the shelf? Which expression represents this?
onLevel.explanation: You are finding how many 1/4-foot pieces fit into 5 feet, which is 5 ÷ 1/4 = 5 × 4 = 20 books.

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Interpret Division of Fractions.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside.

extending.title: Find the Interpretation Error
extending.correctWork: 4 ÷ 1/5 means 'how many 1/5-size pieces fit into 4,' not '1/5 of 4.' The correct answer is 4 × 5 = 20.
hints: Does dividing by 1/5 make the result smaller or larger than 4?
The student confused 'divide by 1/5' with 'multiply by 1/5.' What is the difference?

extending.prompt: Dividing 6 by 1/2 gives 12, but dividing 6 by 2 gives 3. How can dividing by a smaller number (1/2) give a bigger answer than dividing by a larger number (2)? Explain using a real-world example.
extending.sentenceFrame: When I divide 6 by 2, I am ___. When I divide 6 by 1/2, I am ___. The answer is bigger because ___.

extending.label: Complete the pattern: as the unit fraction divisor gets smaller, what happens to the quotient?

optionalActivity.name: Fraction Division Story Match
optionalActivity.intro: Crack the case file: match each detective situation to the fraction-division expression and picture that solves it.

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Interpret Division of Fractions is skipping the key idea: "Dividing by a fraction smaller than 1 gives an answer BIGGER than what you started with, because many tiny pieces fit inside." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: A detective has 6 yards of crime scene tape. She needs to mark off sections that are each 1/3 of a yard. She wants to know how many sections she can mark.
connect.promptQuestion: How does interpreting fraction division help the detective?
connect.prompt: The detective can mark ___ sections because 6 ÷ 1/3 = ___.

exitTicket.stem: What does 5 ÷ 1/4 mean, and what is the quotient?
choices: How many 1/4-size pieces fit into 5; quotient is 20
exitTicket.explanation: 5 ÷ 1/4 means 'how many 1/4-size pieces fit into 5.' Since each whole has 4 fourths, 5 × 4 = 20.

printables.name: Fraction Division Model Match
printables.desc: Students match fraction-division situations to equations and quotients, then explain what each quotient means.

printables.name: Interpret Division of Fractions — Color by Number
printables.desc: Solve each problem, then color by the answer to reveal a picture.
